data.title: V2X: The Surprising Winner Of The Aerospace And Defense Surge
data.description:
V2X (VVX) had a strong start to the year, gaining more than 20% on the back of a possible $1.5 trillion defense budget. The stock is now up 13.5% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500’s 9.3% gain. In this report, I discuss whether the stock still has upside after the recent surge and update my price target in support of my buy rating.

A Surprising Outperformance For V2X

Looking at the performance of V2X, I have to say it's a positive surprise to see V2X outperform aerospace and defense peers. The S&P 500 gained around 2%, so the aerospace and defense industry saw strong outperformance, gaining 16.7%. V2X had an even stronger gain at around 20.1%. Historically, V2X has been an underperformer, and to understand why that is the case, we have to understand the business itself.

V2X Is A Lower-Margin Mission-Support And Services Contractor

V2X is not a company that produces weapon systems. Instead, the company fundamentally is a services and mission-support contractor, which means the company bids for contracts for base operations, logistics, sustainment and training. Generally, this is a business with low margins. V2X has margins of around 7%. The margin used to be around but below 8%. So, the company has struggled to keep margins steady.

Mission-support contractors usually face high costs, inflating the revenues, but the actual profits are not big. Part of the reason for that is not just the high cost nature of the business that V2X is in. One element that limits the margins is the fact that many of these contracts are so called “cost-plus” contracts, which are contracts on which the contract value is structured as cost coverage plus a fee. That means that the risk of higher than anticipated costs remains with the customer, and in exchange, the margin on those contracts is smaller, since the cost risk is also lower. That's a stark contrast from fixed-cost contracts, where operational efficiency defines the margins, commanding an overall higher margin opportunity. V2X typically has cost-plus work for base operations in the Middle East.

Additional pressures include the initial ramp up of contracts as well as winding down programs and recompetes on contracts. A recompete is when an existing contract with the government ends and the government opens up the contract for bidding. Since these often are long-term contracts, mission-support contractors are eager to bid for these contracts since the next opportunity for the same program could be years later, and that means that even if V2X did a terrific job on the first contract, it still has to put in a competitive offer.

A solution for contracts on which V2X is performing well would be to convert the existing or new contracts into fixed-cost contracts. However, that's easier said than done. Remaining on a cost-plus type contract gives the government more space to add scope to the contract, cut risks of underperformance on the contracted work to lower costs and optimize margins, and maintain negotiation strength for recompetes. Additionally, for V2X going with a cost-plus nature may also not be desired due to volatile cost components such as labor, fuel, scope, and subcontractor pricing, which are harder to predict over the longer term. For that reason, margins on the contracts and, consequently, margins for mission-support providers are lower.

For aerospace and defense names, I'm typically looking for margins of 10%-15%. For mission-support contractors, that would be 8%-9%. In some way it's quite surprising to see a lower-margin business see its stock outperform higher-margin peers.

Higher Defense Spending Has an Indirect Benefit for Mission-Support Contractors

That V2X stock benefited as much as it did from the prospects of higher defense spending also does not make a lot of sense if you look at what a higher defense budget would result in. It would definitely not result in higher fees on sustainment. The rule of thumb is that 50% of the life cycle cost of a defense platform goes to R&D and procurement, and the remainder is procurement. That provides another reason as to why sustainment contracts have lower margins. It's where the government can keep some control on cost without compromising on capability developed and procurement quantities.

Typically, for a defense budget, around 40% of the budget is allocated to sustainment. It can be expected that a higher defense budget would be leveraged to advance research and development, as we have seen the share of R&D growth go from the low teens to high teens over the past decade. Since we're currently at a point where tension is high and capabilities and threat levels require advancement in hypersonics, cyber, drone, and missile air defense. So, additional defense funds in the budget would likely be earmarked to advance and accelerate capability development without development programs cannibalizing each other due to budget constraints. Down the line, additional funding would prevent programs from cannibalizing procurement funds. We have seen that F-35 procurement quantities have come down simply because there are not enough dollars available in the budget to procure as initially intended. That means that before we see any strong benefit for mission-support contractors, we will see more funds flowing to the development of new capabilities and accelerating that development and, after that, procurement. Mission-support and services in some way is last in line to benefit, and the benefit would be more in out-years rather than in the near term.

Rather, the benefit from a higher defense budget would come from the potential of having more defense platforms in service and higher quantities of defense equipment in service. All will require sustainment work to be carried out. However, that spike in the defense budget will not substantially alter the “work available” for services contractors, as output will not rise immediately. So, there's a multi-step and multi-year process required for the benefit to flow to service providers. There would only be a direct benefit for companies such as V2X if military activity increases in the near term for a prolonged period, requiring additional maintenance to be carried out. That would be more of a volume-driven growth driver than a margin opportunity.

V2X Remains Attractive Despite Lower Nature Business

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For V2X, I'm adjusting my base case price target from $67.45 to $80.70 as we shift the valuation window from 2025 to 2026. We note that compared to my prior assessment, the target for 2025 was reduced by $0.20, driven by lower expectations on free cash flow. EBITDA estimates have remained stable with a 0.6% upward revision for 2025-2027, indicating 5.5% growth. Free cash flow estimates came down by more than 25% for 2025, driven by higher working capital requirements, and have also come down by 12.5% for this year and almost 10% for next year.

EBITDA is expected to dip in 2025 to around 7% and recover in the years after. We also see the dip back in the free cash flow performance and a higher share of capital expenditures against the operating cash flow. V2X has a $100 million repurchase program, which I believe the company will carry out over the next few years. We also note that the company is expected to grow its cash position in the years ahead and have a leverage below 2x this year. For aerospace and defense companies, I target a net-debt leverage of 2x-2.5x. That means that, in theory, V2X has around $255 million to $455 million to spend on acquisitions by 2027 or return that value to shareholders.

Conclusion: V2X Buy Rating Is Maintained

V2X is the surprising winner of the surge in aerospace and defense stock prices. Looking at the nature of the business, we note that V2X would not be the straightforward winner because a prospective increase in the defense budget would predominantly benefit original equipment manufacturers and not so much the mission-support contractors such as V2X. Nonetheless, we note that with the stock price increase, the company is now more or less fairly valued against 2025 earnings, and additional upside exists for this year, supporting my buy rating.

data.title: L3Harris Technologies: Too Important To Fail, But Not A Buy At Any Price
data.description:
The $1 billion agreement between the U.S. Department of Defense and L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX) represents one of the fundamental drivers that are changing the positive outlook for this company and for the industry. It gives the company a high level of control over the long-term future and also establishes a degree of visibility and a backstop of government support to protect the long-term position of a key asset for this company.

For me there was no question about that. I have owned shares of L3Harris since before this news, and I believe the structural picture of the company has been enhanced. However, the reaction of the market to the announcement of the agreement has been quick. Possibly too quick. The price of the stock is at historic highs, the stock has tremendous momentum (more on that later), and the profit taking is completely normal.

Therefore, while I still believe the stock is a Strong Buy, the timing of entry into the stock is more important than ever.

The Pentagon Agreement

As I previously stated in my August article, I delivered an in-depth overview of the company and its strategic position. For those readers who wish to understand the entire picture of L3Harris's business and its strategic position, I would recommend reading that article again. However, for purposes of this analysis, I want to highlight what is new in the terms of the agreement with the Pentagon and why this agreement is relevant today.

What is significant about the agreement with the Pentagon is not the $1 billion by the Pentagon to L3Harris, but rather the form of the participation of the Pentagon. This is not simply another contract or a supplemental order: the Department of Defense is directly participating, through convertible preferential equity, in the new entity that will be created from the spinoff of the Missile Solutions business. In effect, the Pentagon is partnering with L3Harris as a supplier, in addition to purchasing products from L3Harris.

In other words, the Pentagon will become a partner of L3Harris in the new company. The new company will be focused on producing solid rocket motors for various systems, including Tomahawk, SM-6, Patriot, and THAAD missile defense systems. L3Harris will retain approximately 51-60% of the ownership of the new company and will go public through an IPO in the second half of 2026.

In return for this investment, the Pentagon will receive a high level of visibility related to their requirements for these types of systems. Specifically, the Pentagon will obtain framework contracts with the new company for up to 10 years, and estimates call for the volume of solid rocket motors purchased to range from 600 to 2,000 units annually, which can be translated into terms of the size of the opportunity and the value of the agreement to both parties.

In essence, the Pentagon is saying that this production capacity is so important that it cannot fall prey to the typical ebbs and flows of an industry. The supply chain shortages that occurred as a result of war in Ukraine and the Middle East have made evident just how small the Western industrial base is. This thinking is clearly proactive; invest now to avoid bottlenecks later. With the government involved in the capital, with demand guarantees, and with a project included in the broader framework of the approximately $140 billion "Golden Dome" missile defense project, the risk-return profile of the propulsion business has changed substantially. On paper, it looks very attractive. I may be mistaken, but this is one of the first times I've seen a real "socialization" of risk without eliminating the private capital market. Of course, there will need to be execution, which makes the above somewhat uncomfortable due to potential conflicts of interest and regulatory oversight.

Not Just a Contract

This is something entirely different. The Department of Defense is actually purchasing the capital asset of the manufacturing process by making a $1 billion investment in the form of convertible preferred equity into Missile Solutions; which is the new entity to be spun off from L3Harris' solid rocket motor business. When I refer to Missile Solutions as "too important to fail," I do not mean that the U.S. Government is going to guarantee profitability to Missile Solutions or eliminate the risks associated with its operations; rather, it means that the Department of Defense is stating that solid rocket motor production capability is a strategic variable and that the Department of Defense cannot afford to lose such capability due to insufficient capital, excessive lead time between orders, and/or bottlenecks. Therefore, the Department of Defense has determined to participate in the equity financing of Missile Solutions through the use of convertible instruments. In essence, a separate entity (Missile Solutions) is being established where LHX will retain 51% ownership of the same, Missile Solutions plans to go public via an IPO during the second half of 2026, and until such time as the IPO occurs, Missile Solutions will utilize multi-year capital to expand its industrial base and enhance the reliability of its supply chain.

2026 (1H) Set-up of the new Missile Solutions entity and definition of governance/capex plan
2026 (2H) IPO planned (2H26) + details on shareholder structure and use of proceeds
2027 Ramp-up launch: first effects on production capacity and delivery times
2028-29 Increase in output towards a range of 600–2,000 engines/year; stabilization of the supply chain
2030 More "steady-state" capacity: scaled-up production + longer contractual visibility

To LHX, this is not a near-term increase in [EPS], however, it will provide a change in profile to the company since it will receive increased visibility, increased predictability, and, most importantly, reduced risk of being left behind with respect to structurally high demand, while having limited manufacturing capacity available. I may be mistaken, but I believe this is one of the few instances where I have seen a customer "put their money where their mouth is," so to speak, in order to secure future delivery of product.

A Better Story, But a More Demanding Entry Point

After announcing the story, the stock price immediately shot up and hit a new high. The market responded rapidly, values shifted, and, technically speaking, momentum began to develop. Now let’s get down to business; the better part of the good news has already been factored into the price of the stock.

However, if we look closely at the charts, we can't easily conclude with the usual comment, "You're paying too much." Yes, LHX is trading at a forward EV/EBITDA blended multiple of around 17.5x, significantly above the five-year historical average of just under 13x. It's clear to me that the market is placing greater importance on the visibility and quality of the company's future cash flows than in the past, as I mentioned earlier. This places greater demands on the stock in the short term because historically, similar levels of valuation typically result in a period of consolidation.

On the forward P/E multiple side, however, the situation is somewhat different. According to the data, LHX is still trading at a lower P/E multiple than the average of its peer group, which is trading at higher multiples (see the chart). Therefore, while the stock has become more expensive relative to its own history, it is not the most expensive stock within the sector, which helps to explain why the long term thesis continues to be valid even after the rapid increase in price.

Finally, there is the technical aspect of momentum. The RSI is in the overbought range, which does not indicate that the story itself is weak, but rather that short term timing becomes increasingly difficult. As stated earlier, the market anticipates strong earnings growth, particularly beginning in 2027, when the spinoff is operational and production capacity begins to expand. Thus, it is completely natural for the stock to experience profit taking or to trade laterally until then.

In this regard, it can get very uncomfortable. Being a Strong Buy based on your thesis does not automatically equate to a “buy at any price.” It is quite risky to buy a stock that has already been on a strong upward trend. As such, I believe it makes sense to build your position in a more measured manner, potentially through several tranches, as opposed to attempting to force an immediate entry into a stock that has risen to high levels.

Preview Of Earnings

I believe earnings will be another important catalyst to monitor, although I don't expect any major surprises. Rather than focusing on actual earnings per share (EPS) for the quarter, I'm focusing on the quality of the company's order book and its visibility into the future of its product pipeline.

There are three key points that I will be watching in the upcoming quarterly earnings announcement:

  1. Production Capacity and Ramp-up Times: This is where the impact of the Pentagon deal is expected to start being felt, although the comment is likely to be qualitative (i.e., what the company says regarding production capacity and ramp-up times).
  2. Capital Discipline: How management describes capital expenditures, returns on investment, and priorities in light of the fact that the U.S. Government is becoming an industrial partner to the company.
  3. International Demand: Particularly NATO and export opportunities, which continue to represent a strong structural opportunity with a high degree of non-cyclicality.

At this point, expectations become important. The biggest risk after the recent rally is not a "bad" quarterly earnings report, but rather an average or normal quarterly report. While the market may be looking for immediate evidence of upward momentum in earnings, I believe that as long as there is no damage to medium- to long-term visibility, the noise created by short-term events is less significant.

Risk Factors

  1. Execution Risk of the Spinoff: I believe the process will run smoothly, and we can expect no issues with the implementation of the plan; however, as this is a "binary" event; I am viewing this factor with caution. There are several key factors that need to fall into place before the IPO (scheduled for the second half of 2026): Governance, Timing, Authorizations, Capital Allocation.
  2. Industrial vs. Political/Regulatory Risk: While having the Pentagon as our Partner significantly mitigates industrial risk; it does increase political/regulatory risk. When an organization becomes Strategic Infrastructure, you receive greater scrutiny, potentially limiting your ability to make executive decisions, and decreased flexibility regarding operations.
  3. Timing Risk: Investor sentiment has been raised due to recent rallies. Therefore, a quarterly earnings report that meets the expectations of investors, comments made by management regarding timelines for ramping up to full production, or the need for the company to consolidate its current price levels will create short term volatility. Timing risk will not prevent the long-term thesis from being correct; however, it will test investors who have entered this opportunity without sufficient margin to absorb potential losses.

Conclusion and Rating

All things considered, my view on L3Harris remains unchanged. The long-term thesis has become stronger, not weaker. Visibility has increased from an agreement with the Department of Defense (Pentagon), which also eliminates significant industrial risks to a very important component of their business. Additionally, L3Harris' strategic position relative to the U.S. government and its various agencies in the event of future geopolitical tensions has been enhanced, as the environment is less likely to return to normalcy for some time.

However, the market has acted very aggressively, and the stock is trading at all-time highs, and multiples are high. Therefore, while the opportunity to buy into L3Harris is still available, the required discipline is significantly greater than what was needed six months ago.

Therefore, my rating remains a Strong Buy. I believe that the combination of backlog, the long-term visibility of L3Harris, and the additional optionality provided by the planned spin-off of Missile Solutions supports the valuation premium being applied to the company today. I am maintaining a target price of $383 and anticipate a slow and steady rerating of the stock as Missile Solutions' industrial progress becomes increasingly clear.

data.title: PDI: A 15% Yielding Flagship Ready For The Rate-Cut Cycle
data.description:
Investment thesis

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) closed-end fund is, to my mind, currently one of the best assets for a personal investment portfolio focused on generating passive income. Here, several advantages are combined: high dividend yield (about 15%), wide diversification of the asset portfolio, adaptability to NAV growth during a cycle of falling interest rates, and a constant share price premium. Such characteristics of CEF make it a "Buy" recommendation, particularly if you are looking to diversify your portfolio by adding different types of assets. This article aims to analyze the concept of the PDI investment strategy and identify the main factors for the fund's potential growth in 2026. Also in this article, we will assess dividend safety, showing how the Fed's current monetary policy affects NII and the Coverage Ratio.

PDI's strategy and growth catalysts

The PDI is a closed-end fund whose investment strategy is based on working with debt instruments in the multi-sector market. Its main assets are mortgage-backed securities (MBS), derivatives, and high-yield bonds. According to the fund's prospectus, at least 25% of its assets are invested in private mortgage securities, and up to 40% in securities issued by economically developing countries. The structure of PDI's portfolio is such that 24.40% of its assets are high-yield credit, with 21.82% in non-agency mortgages and 14.94% in non-USD developed securities. In total, there are 2,037 holdings in the portfolio, and the top 10 assets account for 27.19%.

The distinctive feature of such a large-scale PDI portfolio is that it was formed mainly through the acquisition of another fund in the PIMCO family, namely PCI. As a result, a lot of the assets that were sold off and never realized remain in the PDI portfolio. In spite of their low liquidity, these assets were purchased at a very low price. Before the acquisition, most of them had lost around 90-95% of their value. Despite their outdated issue dates, the assets remain stable, creating a kind of “safety cushion.” The low price makes it difficult for these assets to depreciate further. On the contrary, the return on invested capital is quite high, enabling PDI to offer investors a current dividend yield of 14.54%.

In addition to its vast array of assets, PDI's competitive advantage lies in the size of its portfolio. Fund AUM currently stands at $7.20 billion. Taking PDI's two main competitors from the PIMCO family, Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) and PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY), the combined value of their portfolios is 40% less. Not long ago, I compared one of them, the PTY, to its main competitor, the Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund (GOF), where the recommendation is also "Buy." Coming back to the PDI, it’s worth noting that the size of its portfolio is important for attracting large institutional investors. Also, the liquidity in the market for PDI positions lets big money buy without much slippage in the order book. And the broad diversification of the portfolio provides protection against the potential default of one or more issuers, a definite advantage.

The scale of the portfolio requires a complex investment strategy, and in my opinion, that's another advantage of PDI. In addition to broad diversification, they use aggressive hedging through derivatives. The core of the fund's strategy is to aggressively use leverage and interest rate swaps. In periods of predictable interest rate changes, PDI generates excess returns, allowing it to make high payments to shareholders. Within the framework of credit leverage, the fund uses reverse repurchase agreements amounting to 29.62% of the total portfolio, and credit default swaps amounting to 1.42%, where the total effective leverage is 31.04%.

Proof of PDI's high interest among investors is that its shares are trading at a premium to NAV. At the moment, this premium is 7.13%. At the same time, the maximum PDI premium over the past year was on October 1, 2025, with a share price of $19.84 and a NAV of $17.02 (a premium of 14.21%). When I analyzed PDI in my previous article (in comparison with other funds in the PIMCO family), the premium was 4.3%. At that time, I saw it as a good opportunity to buy the asset for my investment portfolio. The share price has since risen by 1.68%, with a total return of 2.94%.

Reasons to invest in PDI in 2026

Now I am confident that PDI is a CEF worth adding to your investment portfolio. Analyzing the fundamental fund on the market and the economic situation in the US, here are the reasons why I recommend PDI as a “Buy”.

Reason #1. The high dividend yield

It's currently at 14.54%, and the average return over the last two years is around 14%. This yield previously reached an average of 17.81% in 2023. Such payments make PDI one of the most profitable funds with high performance and no significant capital erosion. Payments are made monthly, something that is very important for many income-oriented investors.

Reason #2. The cycle of rate cuts

Right now, Fed policy is all about lowering rates, and that's driving up the prices of bonds in PDI's portfolio. This has a positive impact on potential NAV growth. In addition, the financial stability of PDI, which is a CEF that actively uses leverage (31.04%), is dependent on interest rates in the credit market. And given the Fed's current cycle of rate cuts, there is also a decrease in the cost of servicing the leverage itself. This means a potential increase in profits for the fund.

Reason #3. Dynamics of P/NAV

With a current premium of 7.13%, buying PDI seems like a good deal. In general, such a moderate premium reflects the high assessment of investors who believe in the fund's portfolio profitability. Moreover, the total NAV return over the past year was more than 15%, demonstrating the effectiveness of portfolio managers in earning money on the market, and not just paying dividends from the capital.

Reason #4. Quality active management

I see the fact that the PDI portfolio remains unchanged not as a disadvantage, but as an advantage. It invests in a wide range of instruments, the majority of which are bonds and MBS. The portfolio's structure, in terms of weight, may change. Such a flexible approach allows it to adapt to changes in interest rates in order to maintain its high level of profitability. Asset duration averages more than three years. And buying them at an undervalued price generates the profits needed to pay dividends. What's more, PDI invests in assets that lock in previous high rates, allowing investments to become more profitable.

PDI dividend safety analysis: it's higher than you think

There is nothing more interesting to investors than whether the yield of around 15% is justified and whether it can be maintained given the current cycle of interest rate cuts. According to many, however, the size of net investment income could be affected. Furthermore, should it prove insufficient for current payments, the fund would have to use its own capital (RoC). This would cause erosion, and in my opinion, it would be imprudent for a fund such as PDI. Based on reports, Distribution Coverage Ratio will stabilize by the end of 2025. That's a good sign that the fund will be able to pay dividends to shareholders even without using its own capital.

PDI's leverage ratio also affects dividend security in 2026. Please note that the current Total Effective Leverage ratio is 31.04%, the main instrument being Reverse Repurchase Agreements. These instruments are linked to the SOFR rate. Therefore, every time the Fed cuts rates, repurchase rates also fall sharply. This results in a reduction in the cost of borrowing for the fund in a relatively short period of time. The SOFR rate is currently 3.64%, while at the beginning of September 2025 it was 4.39%. In absolute terms, the rate has fallen by 0.75%. As a percentage, that's a 17.08% drop. This means that PDI's borrowing costs could go down by about 17%, giving a nice boost to its margins. After all, many of its portfolio assets (especially MBS) have long durations. I think this is a strong internal catalyst for NAV growth and makes payments more reliable in the coming months.

When analyzing the data for 2025 in the Coverage Ratio section, note that PDI is going through a phase of restoring profitability. The coverage ratio fell to a critical 25.16% in May 2025. Since then, though, it's been steadily recovering, growing from $0.055 to $0.153. Payout coverage itself increased to 69.67% (latest data for November 2025). Growth in NII in the second half of 2025 coincides with changes in the Fed's monetary policy, underscoring the importance of the impact of lower borrowing costs through effective use of leverage. In addition, while the latest UNII figure is negative (-$0.45), investors should not be alarmed, since the fund managers are skillfully employing a derivatives strategy. This generates additional income that is not always reflected in the NII column.
